title: Terence Tao's ChatGPT conversation about the Jacobian Conjecture counterexample
titleZh: 陶哲轩关于雅可比猜想反例的 ChatGPT 对话
author: gmays
relevanceReason: 命中 3 个 AI/科技关键词。
textRawHtml:
A digestion of the Jacobian conjecture counterexample - https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48998362 - July 2026 (133 comments)

Claude Fable produced a counterexample to the Jacobian Conjecture - https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48973869 - July 2026 (508 comments)
textZhHtml:
雅可比猜想反例的消化 - https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48998362 - 2026 年 7 月（133 条评论）

克劳德·寓言提出了雅可比猜想的反例 - https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48973869 - 2026 年 7 月（508 条评论）
summaryRaw: Get responses tailored to youLog in to get answers based on saved chats, plus create images and upload files.
summaryZh: Get responses tailored to youLog in to get answers based on saved chats, plus create images and upload files.

comments.author: lukebuehler
comments.textRawHtml: It’s endlessly fascinating to read the AI transcript of an expert who _really_ knows how to cut to the chase. It just shows how much you can potentially squeeze out of these models. I’m also surprised to see that even Terrence Tao seems to use it in a way that resembles, in progression, how I use llms in my area of expertise (emphasis on progression and usage patterns, not absolute skill, obv I don’t match that): short pointed questions that goes all in on the jargon and machinery of the field and steers the llm hard (eg no softballs). I’ve noticed that llms switch their tone and meet you basically more or less on your level.
comments.textZhHtml: 阅读一位_真正_知道如何切入正题的专家的 AI 成绩单是一件非常令人着迷的事情。它只是显示了您可以从这些模型中榨取多少潜力。我还惊讶地发现，就连陶哲轩也以一种类似的方式使用它，在进展中，我如何在我的专业领域使用LLM（强调进展和使用模式，而不是绝对技能，显然我不匹配）：简短而尖锐的问题，全部涉及该领域的行话和机制，并努力引导LLM（例如，没有垒球）。我注意到，LLMS 会改变他们的语气，基本上会在你的水平上或多或少地与你见面。

children.author: michaelchisari
children.textRawHtml:
It reinforces how to "learn AI" is to first master the problem domain.

I can use AI for coding after decades of coding. I can't use it for theoretical physics because I can't evaluate the responses.
children.textZhHtml:
它强调了如何“学习人工智能”首先要掌握问题领域。

经过几十年的编码，我可以使用人工智能进行编码。我无法将其用于理论物理，因为我无法评估响应。

children.author: larsfaye
children.textRawHtml:
Correctamundo. That's EXACTLY what I just wrote about. And how we'll create the next generation of people who know how to evaluate responses when we're creating a generation of people who are increasingly reliant on LLMs to do the work makes for a strange paradox.

https://larsfaye.com/articles/ai-coding-will-prevent-experti...
children.textZhHtml:
纠正。这正是我刚刚写的。当我们培养越来越依赖法学硕士来完成工作的下一代人时，我们如何培养知道如何评估反应的下一代人，这就形成了一个奇怪的悖论。

https://larsfaye.com/articles/ai-coding-will-prevent-experti...

children.author: jeef_berky
children.textRawHtml:
It's so obvious to some and may never be considered by others.

If someone asks about AI I tell them step 1 is ask it how to do something you know all about. Step 2 is consider everything else you ask will be that inaccurate.
children.textZhHtml:
这对某些人来说是显而易见的，但其他人可能永远不会考虑。

如果有人询问人工智能，我告诉他们第一步是问它如何做你所知道的事情。第 2 步是考虑您提出的其他所有问题都是不准确的。

children.author: codebolt
children.textRawHtml: As a dev with three decades experience writing code, I use it for stuff I know all about all the time. And these days, the majority of the time it is more correct than I would've been without a few iterations of trial and error.
children.textZhHtml: 作为一名拥有三十年代码编写经验的开发人员，我一直将其用于我所知道的事情。如今，大多数时候它比我没有经过几次反复试验的情况下更正确。

children.author: rnd0
children.textRawHtml: Honestly that seems kind of pointless and a waste of time. Might as well work it out by hand if you're going to go through all that effort
children.textZhHtml: 老实说，这似乎毫无意义，而且浪费时间。如果您要付出所有这些努力，不妨手工解决

children.author: tdeck
children.textRawHtml: GP is saying that when you use it in a domain where you're familiar, you'll naturally spot bad or lazy approaches and heavily steer the LLM, so you should keep that in mind when using it in domains you aren't an expert in (and thus can't as easily spot those things). I'm not sure why the tone of your reply is so uncharitable.
children.textZhHtml: GP 表示，当您在熟悉的领域使用它时，您自然会发现糟糕或懒惰的方法，并严重指导 LLM，因此在您不是专家的领域使用它时（因此无法轻易发现这些事情），您应该记住这一点。我不知道为什么你的回复语气如此不友善。

children.author: Kon5ole
children.textRawHtml:
Good writeup, and certainly food for thought.

I wonder if this key point actually holds though:

>The skills to do so, however, are a function of someone who has experienced the friction and challenges over time that culminate in "good taste".

It's certainly sometimes true, but I don't think it's a general rule. Sometimes friction is just friction and sometimes you spend 1000 hours learning something that disappears and becomes obsolete or at least irrelevant to the goal.

Programmers used to need to know the instruction set of the CPU, assembly language and so on. Some still do but for most developers today that's not useful. Everything you know about 6800 assembly will not make your note-taking app any better.

I think we are in a state where AI tools so easily mimic what we used to do by hand that we think the friction is gone, but that's because we haven't raised the bar yet. One day we'll look at the Fable one-shot that's better than anything we ever made by hand ourselves and say "It could be even better".

And then the friction is back, just on a new level.
children.textZhHtml:
写得很好，当然值得深思。

我想知道这个关键点是否真的成立：

>然而，这样做的技能是一个经历过长期摩擦和挑战并最终达到“良好品味”的人的函数。

这有时确实是正确的，但我不认为这是一条普遍规则。有时摩擦只是摩擦，有时你花了 1000 小时学习一些东西，但这些东西消失了，变得过时了，或者至少与目标无关。

程序员过去需要了解 CPU 的指令集、汇编语言等。有些仍然这样做，但对于今天的大多数开发人员来说，这没有用。您所了解的关于 6800 组装的所有知识都不会让您的笔记应用程序变得更好。

我认为我们正处于这样一种状态：人工智能工具可以轻松模仿我们过去用手做的事情，我们认为摩擦已经消失，但那是因为我们还没有提高标准。有一天，我们会看到比我们自己亲手制作的任何作品都更好的《神鬼寓言》，并说“它可能会更好”。

然后摩擦又回来了，只是达到了一个新的水平。

children.author: smsm42
children.textRawHtml:
> Everything you know about 6800 assembly will not make your note-taking app any better.

It could. It could make a simple note-taking app not take gigabytes of memory and take visible delay on each click. Most people don't bother of course because simple note-taking app is not worth the effort. It's possible to do better, it's just often not practical.
children.textZhHtml:
> 您所了解的关于 6800 组装的一切并不会让您的笔记应用程序变得更好。

可以的。它可以使一个简单的笔记应用程序不占用千兆字节的内存，并且每次点击都会出现明显的延迟。大多数人当然不会打扰，因为简单的笔记应用程序不值得付出努力。可以做得更好，但通常不切实际。

children.author: rkuodys
children.textRawHtml: I am not sure if I'm ignorant or have too much self esteem, but in cases where domain is not my expertise I try to cross challenge using different models to get less-biased and hopefully more correct insights.
children.textZhHtml: 我不确定自己是否无知或太自尊，但在领域不是我的专业知识的情况下，我会尝试使用不同的模型来应对挑战，以获得更少偏见并希望获得更正确的见解。

children.author: beng-nl
children.textRawHtml:
I’m not trying to be snarky, but.. I wouldn’t try to upgrade an answer from a stranger you can’t fully trust to an expert answer you can trust by asking more strangers who have all only read the same books and use the same system for summarizing the books.

Similarly, sometimes I have to manually validate the output of an ai tool, analyzed over a large text I can’t practically read and understand, and best way I’ve found is to ask the tool (or another ai) to ‘show your work,’ ie make it help me make the determination by showing places in the text I have to read to follow its reasoning. We can never trust another ai directly to assess the validity of another ai.
children.textZhHtml:
我并不是想刻薄，但是..我不会试图通过询问更多只读过相同书籍并使用相同系统来总结书籍的陌生人，将您不能完全信任的陌生人的答案升级为您可以信任的专家答案。

同样，有时我必须手动验证人工智能工具的输出，对我实际上无法阅读和理解的大文本进行分析，我发现的最好方法是要求该工具（或另一个人工智能）“展示你的作品”，即让它通过显示我必须阅读的文本中的位置来遵循其推理来帮助我做出决定。我们永远不能直接信任另一个人工智能来评估另一个人工智能的有效性。

children.author: gamerDude
children.textRawHtml:
This is some work that I want to see in education. How do you validate if something is true or not.

A lot of times that will take physical tests. Or in the case of math/logic, tests to validate each line or validated sources of previously proved theorems
children.textZhHtml:
这是我想在教育领域看到的一些作品。如何验证某件事是否属实。

很多时候需要进行物理测试。或者在数学/逻辑的情况下，进行测试以验证每一行或验证先前证明的定理的来源

children.author: satvikpendem
children.textRawHtml: The paradox is analogous to AI poisoning its own training data as more and more AI generated content is released on the Internet. Indeed, I see a hard terminus for both man and machine at some point.
children.textZhHtml: 这个悖论类似于随着越来越多的人工智能生成的内容在互联网上发布，人工智能毒害了自己的训练数据。事实上，我认为人类和机器在某个时刻都面临着一个艰难的终点站。

children.author: dnemmers
children.textRawHtml: Absolutely right. First gen models are trained on 'virgin' non-LLM output. Subsequent models are tainted by ingesting AI replies. Rinse and repeat, and all you end up with is AI copies of AI replies, and the noise will completely overtake the signal.
children.textZhHtml: 完全正确。第一代模型是在“原始”非 LLM 输出上进行训练的。随后的模型因摄取人工智能回复而受到污染。冲洗并重复，你最终得到的只是人工智能回复的人工智能副本，噪音将完全超过信号。

children.author: literalAardvark
children.textRawHtml:
I'd have been more worried about that had the models not gotten incredibly good over time.

But they did get good and this seems like a non-issue.
children.textZhHtml:
如果模型没有随着时间的推移变得非常好，我会更担心这一点。

但它们确实变得很好，这似乎不是问题。

children.author: pbronez
children.textRawHtml:
Great article… been worrying about this one quite a bit:

> If these tools demand expertise, yet the tools can actively circumvent the friction that cultivates expertise, then what is the path for one to become an expert so they can effectively use these tools?
children.textZhHtml:
很棒的文章......一直在担心这个问题：

>如果这些工具需要专业知识，但这些工具可以主动规避培养专业知识的摩擦，那么一个人如何成为专家才能有效地使用这些工具？
